originalTitle: UN votes to recognise enslavement of Africans as 'gravest crime against humanity'
title: Big News! The World Says 'Sorry' to Africa
content:
Hello, my amazing friends! Your friendly news reporter from Africa here. I have some very important news to share with you today! It's about something that happened very far away, in a place called the United Nations. This news is special for all of us here in Africa, and for people everywhere!

What is the United Nations?

First, let's talk about the United Nations, or UN for short. Imagine a super big club, where almost all the countries in the world are members! It's like a big family meeting where leaders from different countries come together. They talk about big problems and try to find ways to make the world a better, safer, and fairer place for everyone. They want peace and happiness for all children and grown-ups.

A Very Sad Story From Long Ago

Now, for the important news. The UN just talked about something very, very sad that happened a long, long time ago. It was called the enslavement of Africans. Imagine people living happily in their homes in Africa. Then, some bad people came and took them away from their families and their land. They forced these people to get on big ships and travel across the ocean. They were taken to other countries and made to work very, very hard for free. They had no freedom, no choice, and no way to go home. This terrible thing lasted for many, many years. It was a time when millions of African people suffered greatly. This is what we call slavery.

Calling it the 'Gravest Crime'

The UN had a big meeting and decided something very important. They said that taking African people and forcing them into slavery was the "gravest crime against humanity." "Gravest crime" means it was one of the worst, most unfair, and most cruel things that has ever happened in the history of the world. "Against humanity" means it was bad for all people, not just a few. It hurt so many people and took away their basic human rights. Did you know that "humanity" means all the people in the world? So, a "crime against humanity" means something that hurts everyone's sense of right and wrong, and makes the world a sadder place.

Why Say This Now?

You might be thinking, "But this happened so long ago!" That's true. But even though it was a long time ago, the sadness and the unfairness of it still affect people today. The UN wants everyone to remember this history so that such a terrible thing never, ever happens again to anyone, anywhere in the world. It's about learning from the past.

Asking for an Apology

One big thing the UN said is that people and countries who did these bad things should say "sorry." An apology means saying you are truly sorry for hurting someone or doing something wrong. Saying sorry is important because it shows you understand the pain you caused. It's a way to start healing and moving forward, even if the wrong happened a long time ago. Did you know that saying "sorry" can be a very powerful step towards friendship and understanding? It helps to mend broken hearts and make things right again.

A Special Helping Fund

The UN also talked about something called a "reparations fund." Imagine if someone broke your toy, and then bought you a new one or helped you fix it. That's a bit like what a reparations fund is. It's a special pot of money that some countries might contribute to. This money would be used to help people whose families were affected by slavery a long, long time ago. It's not about making everything perfect, because you can't undo the past. But it's about trying to help make things a little better for people today, to help them build stronger communities and brighter futures.

Building a Brighter Future

This big decision by the UN is very important for Africa. It means that the world is finally recognizing the deep hurt and unfairness that happened to our ancestors. It's like the world is saying, "We see you, Africa. We remember your pain, and we want to help make things right." This helps us to feel stronger and prouder about our history and our beautiful continent. It helps us to teach everyone about fairness and respect. Did you know that Africa is a continent with 54 different countries? Each one has its own special culture, music, and stories!

Learning and Growing Together

This news reminds us all to be kind to each other. It teaches us that everyone deserves freedom, respect, and to be treated fairly, no matter where they come from or what their skin colour is. When we learn about history, both the good and the sad parts, we grow wiser. We learn how to build a world where everyone can live happily and safely. So, that's the big news from the UN! Let's keep learning, keep being kind, and keep building a wonderful future for Africa and for all children around the world. Until next time, my friends!
summary: A big group of countries called the United Nations has said that taking people from Africa and forcing them into slavery a long time ago was a very bad crime, and they are asking for an apology and ways to help heal the past.
whyMatters: This is important because it helps everyone understand a sad part of history, says sorry for big wrongs, and helps to build a fairer and kinder world where everyone is treated with respect and nobody ever has to go through such pain again.

originalTitle: South Africa's police chief to appear in court over controversial health contract
title: Big News from South Africa: The Police Boss and the Rules!
content:
Hello, amazing little explorers! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, bringing you some important news from our wonderful continent of Africa, all the way from sunny South Africa! We're going to talk about something that might sound a bit grown-up, but it's really about rules and fairness, just like in your games at school. Let's learn together!

Welcome, Little Explorers!

Welcome, welcome! I'm so glad you're here. Today, we have a story from a big country called South Africa. It's a land full of beautiful animals like lions and elephants, and big, busy cities. Sometimes, even in big, busy places, things happen that make people ask questions. That's what news is all about – helping us understand our world in a simple way.

Meet the South African Police Boss!

In South Africa, just like in many places, there's a person who is the 'big boss' of all the police. We call him the Police Chief. Think of him like the captain of a very important team! The police are super important because they help keep everyone safe. They make sure people follow the rules, help if someone is lost, and generally make sure our communities are good places to live. It's a very serious and important job! Did you know? South Africa is sometimes called the 'Rainbow Nation' because it has so many different kinds of people and cultures living together! Because the Police Chief has such a big job, he has to make many important decisions. And with big decisions, there are always lots of rules to follow. These rules help make sure everything is fair and right for everyone. Like when you play a game, you have rules, right? So everyone can have fun and play fairly.

What's a 'Health Contract'?

Now, let's talk about something called a 'health contract.' It sounds complicated, but let's make it simple. Imagine your school needs to buy a lot of new books, or maybe new playground equipment. The grown-ups at school would make a special agreement with a company to buy these things. This special agreement is like a 'contract.' In this story, the 'contract' was for health things. Think about doctors, hospitals, medicines, or special masks to keep people healthy and safe. This 'health contract' means there was a big plan to buy lots of important health supplies. But some people are saying that maybe the rules for buying these things weren't followed properly, or that it wasn't fair. This is why people are calling it 'controversial' – it means people are talking about it a lot, and some are not happy about how it happened. Did you know? Doctors and nurses work very hard to keep us all healthy. They are like superheroes without capes! So, this 'health contract' is all about making sure that money meant for keeping people healthy is spent correctly and fairly. It's like making sure all your friends get an equal share of snacks, or that the money for new school books actually buys the best books for everyone.

Heading to Court!

Because there are questions about this 'health contract,' the Police Chief is going to a special place called a court. What is a court? It's a big building where special grown-ups, called judges, listen to stories from people. It's like a very serious meeting where everyone gets to talk about what happened, especially if someone is accused of not following the rules. When someone goes to court because there are questions about whether they followed the rules, we say they are facing 'charges.' The grown-ups in South Africa haven't told us exactly what the Police Chief is accused of doing wrong yet. They are still figuring out all the details, which is okay. It means they are being very careful. Did you know? Even the most important people in a country have to follow the rules, and sometimes they have to go to court to explain things, just like anyone else! In court, the judge listens carefully to everyone. It's not about being angry, but about finding out the truth and making sure that justice is done. Justice means making things fair and right for everyone. So, the Police Chief will go to court to talk about the 'health contract' and answer questions.

Why Are Rules So Important?

Rules are super important, aren't they? Think about the rules at home, like cleaning your room, or the rules at school, like not running in the hallways. Rules keep us safe, help us get along, and make sure things are fair. When leaders, like the Police Chief, have big jobs, it's even more important that they follow all the rules. When leaders follow the rules, it helps everyone in the country trust them. It shows that they care about doing things the right way and using money for things like health in the best way for everyone. Following rules helps a country be strong and happy, like a team that works well together. Did you know? Many countries in Africa have their own special courts and laws to make sure everyone is treated fairly!

What Happens Next?

Going to court takes time. The judge will listen to everything very carefully. People will talk and share their stories. It's like a puzzle, and they need to find all the pieces to see the whole picture. For now, we know the Police Chief will be going to court to talk about this 'health contract.' We will have to wait patiently to hear more about what happens. It's all about figuring out the truth and making sure that everything is fair. If something wasn't done correctly, the court will help decide what needs to happen to make it right again. It's a big process, but it's how we make sure our countries are fair and honest places to live.

Let's Stay Kind!

Even when there are big problems or questions, it's important for us to stay kind and patient. Learning about the news helps us understand the world around us. Thank you for listening, little news explorers! Keep being curious and kind!
summary: The Police Chief in South Africa will go to a special court to talk about a 'health contract' that people are asking questions about, to make sure everything was fair and followed the rules.
whyMatters: This is important because it shows that even big leaders have to follow rules and explain their actions in court, making sure everyone is treated fairly and money meant for important things like health is used correctly for all people in South Africa.

originalTitle: Senegal appeal to be heard 'as swiftly as possible'
title: Senegal appeal to be heard 'as swiftly as possible'
content: The Court of Arbitration for Sport (Cas) says it will rule on Senegal's appeal against being stripped of the Africa Cup of Nations title "as swiftly as possible".

originalTitle: Tehran issues warning to regional neighbour if Iranian island occupied
title: Big Talks About Islands Far, Far Away!
content:
Hello, my wonderful friends from all over Africa! I’m your kid-friendly reporter, bringing you news from around the world. Today, we're going to talk about something happening in a land far, far away, called Iran. It’s a place with sunny deserts and busy cities, a long way from our beautiful homes.

Where is Iran?

Iran is a big country in a part of the world called Asia. It’s very, very far from Africa, across oceans and many other countries. Imagine looking at a big map of the world! Iran would be on one side, and Africa would be on another. People there speak a different language, and they have their own special foods and traditions. Did you know? Iran is one of the oldest countries in the world! People have lived there for thousands and thousands of years, building amazing things and telling wonderful stories.

What is an Island?

Before we go on, let's remember what an island is. An island is a piece of land that has water all around it. Like a little hat floating in a big bath! Some islands are tiny, and some are very big, with towns and people living on them. Countries often own islands, just like you own your favorite toy or book. Did you know? There are so many islands in the world! Some are warm and tropical, with palm trees and sandy beaches. Others are cold and rocky, with lots of interesting animals like penguins!

Who Owns the Land?

Just like you own your clothes or your room, countries own their land. They have borders, which are like invisible lines that show where one country stops and another one starts. These borders help everyone know which land belongs to whom. Sometimes, countries can own islands that are far from their main land, out in the big blue sea.

A Big Warning About an Island

So, what's happening in Iran? Well, a very important grown-up in Iran, someone who speaks for the whole country, gave a big warning. A warning is like when your mum says, "Don't touch that hot stove!" It means something important and serious. This grown-up said that if another country tried to take an island that belongs to Iran, Iran would be very upset and might do something about it. Imagine if someone tried to take your favorite toy without asking! You would feel sad or angry, wouldn't you? That's a bit like how Iran feels about its islands. They want to make sure everyone knows these islands belong to them.

Talking About Peace

Now, there’s another big country, called the United States of America, or USA for short. The grown-ups in charge there say they want everyone in the world to be friends and live in peace. Peace means no fighting, just people getting along and helping each other. It’s like when you play nicely with your friends in the playground. Did you know? There's a special day called the International Day of Peace every year! On this day, people all over the world think about how we can all live together without fighting.

Soldiers and Helping Hands

Sometimes, countries have soldiers. Soldiers are like special protectors. They wear uniforms and train to keep their country safe. The USA has also sent more of its soldiers to the area near Iran. This can be a bit confusing, right? They want peace, but they also send soldiers. It’s a bit like when you have a problem with a friend. You want to talk it out, but sometimes you might also bring an older brother or sister to help make sure everyone is fair. The soldiers are there, often, to help keep things calm and safe for everyone, especially if there are disagreements. Did you know? Soldiers also help people in other ways, like helping when there are big floods or other emergencies! They can build bridges and help deliver food and water.

Why Do Countries Disagree?

Sometimes, grown-ups in different countries don't agree on everything. It can be about who owns a piece of land, or how to share resources, or even just different ideas about how to do things. It's just like how sometimes you and your friends might disagree about which game to play. But it’s always best to talk about it and find a way to be fair. Did you know? There are special people called diplomats who travel between countries just to talk and help solve problems peacefully! They are like super-communicators for countries.

Being Good Neighbours

Even if countries are far apart, they are all neighbours on our big planet Earth. Being a good neighbour means talking nicely, sharing, and trying to understand each other. This story reminds us that it's always important for countries to talk to each other and try to solve problems with words, not with big fights. It’s good that people are talking about peace, even when there are worries about islands and soldiers. We all hope that everyone can be friends and share our wonderful world peacefully.

What Does This Mean for Us?

This big news is happening very far away, and people are working hard to make sure everyone is safe and happy. For us, here in Africa, it’s a good reminder that talking is always better than fighting. Let’s remember to be kind to our friends and neighbours, and hope for peace for everyone, everywhere! And that’s your news from your kid-friendly reporter!
summary: A big country called Iran made a warning about protecting its islands if another country tries to take them, while another country, the USA, talks about peace but also sends soldiers to the area.
whyMatters: It's important because countries are trying to solve disagreements about who owns land and sea, and everyone hopes they can talk nicely and stay friends without fighting.

title: Trump says Iranian leaders want deal ‘so badly’ but fear retaliation
content: Donald Trump said Iran is negotiating with the US but is “afraid to say it”.

title: Northern Ireland deserve Italy's respect - Buffon
content: World Cup winner Gianluigi Buffon says Italy have been fully concentrated on overcoming Northern Ireland as the sides prepare to face each other in Bergamo on Thursday.
